findings.evidence: COMMON_COMMAND = 'bash,-c,source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh && ' followed by string concatenation of params.getUser(), params.getName(), params.getOutput(), params.getCmd(), params.getStartTime(), params.getEndTime(). buildCmdCommand() appends '-cmd ' + params.getCmd(). The result is split(',') and passed to ProcessBuilder.
data_flow_path: CollectionTask.buildCmdCommand(params) -> concatenates params.getUser(), params.getName(), params.getOutput(), params.getCmd(), params.getStartTime(), params.getEndTime() into shell command string
findings.title: Command Injection via bash -c in CollectionTask (cmd collection)
findings.message: CollectionTask.buildCmdCommand concatenates HTTP-controlled fields from CollectorCmdParams (user, name, output, cmd, startTime, endTime) directly into a shell command string executed via ProcessBuilder with 'bash -c'. The 'cmd' field is the most dangerous as it explicitly represents a command to execute. No sanitization is applied. Shell metacharacters in any parameter enable arbitrary command injection.

findings.evidence: buildLogCommand() appends '-user ' + params.getUser(), '-name ' + params.getName(), '-path ' + joinedPaths, '-output ' + params.getOutput(), '-start_time ' + params.getStartTime(), '-end_time ' + params.getEndTime(), '-data_type ' + params.getDataType(). The resulting string is split(',') and passed to ProcessBuilder.
data_flow_path: CollectionTask.buildLogCommand(params) -> concatenates params.getUser(), params.getName(), params.getPaths(), params.getOutput(), params.getStartTime(), params.getEndTime() into shell command string
findings.title: Command Injection via bash -c in CollectionTask (log collection)
findings.message: CollectionTask.executeLogCollection uses ProcessBuilder with 'bash -c' to execute Python log collection script. HTTP-controlled fields from CollectorLogParams (user, name, output, paths, startTime, endTime, dataType) are directly concatenated into a shell command string without sanitization. Shell metacharacters in any parameter enable arbitrary command injection.

findings.evidence: buildTraceCommand() appends '-output ' + params.getOutput(), '-start_time ' + params.getStartTime(), '-end_time ' + params.getEndTime(), '-product_name ' + params.getProductName(), '-field ' + params.getField(). The resulting string is split(',') and passed to ProcessBuilder.
data_flow_path: CollectionTask.buildTraceCommand(params) -> concatenates params.getOutput(), params.getStartTime(), params.getEndTime(), params.getProductName(), params.getField() into shell command string
findings.title: Command Injection via bash -c in CollectionTask (trace collection)
findings.message: CollectionTask.executeTraceCollection uses ProcessBuilder with 'bash -c' to execute Python trace collection script. HTTP-controlled fields from CollectorTraceParams (output, startTime, endTime, productName, field) are directly concatenated into a shell command string without sanitization. Shell metacharacters in any parameter enable arbitrary command injection.

findings.evidence: buildHeapCollectionCommand returns: 'source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh && /opt/oss/manager/apps/DPFaultHorizonService/tool/heap/collect_heap.sh --type heap --name ' + taskRequest.getName() + ' --user ' + taskRequest.getUser() + ' --times ' + taskRequest.getTimes() + ' --interval ' + taskRequest.getInterval(). executeHeapCollectionCommand then runs: new ProcessBuilder('/bin/bash', '-c', cmd).redirectErrorStream(true).start().
data_flow_path: buildHeapCollectionCommand concatenates taskRequest.getName(), taskRequest.getUser() into bash command string
findings.title: Command Injection via bash -c in ScenariosCollectionServiceDelegateImpl (heap collection)
findings.message: ScenariosCollectionServiceDelegateImpl.buildHeapCollectionCommand() directly concatenates HTTP-controlled fields (name, user, times, interval) from CollectorHeapParams into a bash command string. The resulting command is executed via ProcessBuilder('/bin/bash', '-c', cmd). Shell metacharacters in user or name fields enable arbitrary command injection.

findings.evidence: ProactiveInterventionInterfaceDelegateImpl.isolateOpenGemin() calls RuntimeExec.exec(('bash ' + JOB_OPEN_GEMIN).split(' ')) at line 49. isolateKafka() calls RuntimeExec.exec(('bash ' + JOB_KAFKA).split(' ')) at line 90. switchoverDb() calls RuntimeExec.exec(tempCommand) at line 72. No MutilUserAuthCheck or any auth call exists in this class.
findings.title: ProactiveInterventionInterface endpoints execute shell commands without any authentication or authorization
findings.message: ProactiveInterventionInterfaceDelegateImpl executes shell commands via RuntimeExec.exec() for isolateOpenGemin (bash open_gemin_isolation.sh), isolateKafka (bash stop_kafka_service.sh), and switchoverDb (with dynamic DB switchover commands). None of these methods call MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps() or any other authentication/authorization check. Combined with the missing global auth filter, these endpoints are fully accessible to unauthenticated attackers, allowing them to trigger Kafka isolation, OpenGemin isolation, and database switchover operations.

findings.evidence: 1) DPFaultRequestInterfaceDelegateImpl.innerRequest() at line 80-82 calls doRequest(requestParam) directly with no auth check. 2) checkRequestMethod() at line 131 uses ConfigUtils.getErWhileList().stream().noneMatch(requestUrl::contains) - a weak substring contains() check. 3) distributeInstances() at line 147-149 passes requestParam.getUrl() directly to BackendErClient.RequestParam constructor. 4) BackendRestClient.invokeMethod() at line 97-99 calls createAdminHeader() which injects x-user-name=admin, x-user-id=1, x-user-roles=1&2. 5) Whitelist entries from subhealth.properties are path fragments like /rest/dpfault/v1/check that can appear anywhere in the attacker URL.
data_flow_path: BackendRestClient.invokeMethod() -> RestAdapter.get/post/put/delete(url, params, options) [outbound HTTP request with user-controlled URL and admin credentials]
findings.title: SSRF via innerRequest with user-controlled URL and no authentication - admin credentials auto-injected
findings.message: The /dpfault/tool/inner/request endpoint accepts a user-controlled URL via the RequestParam.url field and forwards the request via BackendErClient.invokeRequest() -> BackendRestClient.invokeMethod(). The innerRequest method has NO method-level authentication check. The whitelist check in checkRequestMethod() uses String.contains() which can be bypassed (e.g., http://evil.com/rest/dpfault/v1/check). Additionally, BackendRestClient.invokeMethod() automatically injects admin credentials (x-user-name: admin, x-user-id: 1, x-user-roles: 1&2) into every forwarded request that lacks an x-user-name header, upgrading SSRF from 'can make requests' to 'can make authenticated admin requests to internal APIs'.

findings.evidence: 1) SSRF entry: innerRequest() at line 80 has no auth check. 2) Whitelist bypass: checkRequestMethod() at line 131 uses noneMatch(requestUrl::contains) - URL http://internal-host/rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb contains the whitelisted fragment /rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb. 3) Admin credential injection: BackendRestClient.createAdminHeader() at line 86-91 injects x-user-name=admin, x-user-id=1, x-user-roles=1&2. 4) Command execution target: ProactiveInterventionInterfaceDelegateImpl.switchoverDb() at line 72 calls RuntimeExec.exec(tempCommand), and isolateKafka() at line 90 calls RuntimeExec.exec(('bash ' + JOB_KAFKA).split(' ')). Both endpoints lack method-level auth checks.
data_flow_path: doRequest() -> checkRequestMethod() [contains() whitelist bypass: URL contains /rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb]
Outbound HTTP POST to internal host /rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb with admin credentials
findings.title: Unauthenticated SSRF to internal command execution endpoints via innerRequest with admin credential auto-injection
findings.message: The unauthenticated innerRequest SSRF endpoint can be chained with internal command execution endpoints. The SSRF whitelist uses contains() matching against path fragments including /rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb and /rest/dpfault/v1/isolateKafka. An attacker can craft a URL like http://internal-host/rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb which passes the contains() whitelist check (the URL contains the whitelisted fragment). BackendRestClient.createAdminHeader() then auto-injects admin credentials (x-user-name: admin, x-user-id: 1, x-user-roles: 1&2), so the forwarded request arrives at the command execution endpoint with full admin privileges. The switchoverDb endpoint executes RuntimeExec.exec() with dynamic database switchover commands, and isolateKafka executes bash stop_kafka_service.sh. This chain gives an unauthenticated attacker the ability to trigger critical infrastructure operations (database switchover, Kafka isolation) via SSRF with auto-injected admin credentials.

findings.evidence: web.xml maps only CommonFilter and WebContextFilter to /*. No filter-class matching AuthorizeFilter, AuthFilter, SecurityFilter, or URLAuthFilter is present. FilterSupport.isEnableLocal() returns false because the 'enable_local' file does not exist in pub/Bus/. No Spring Security (SecurityFilterChain, WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter) or HandlerInterceptor is configured anywhere in the project.
data_flow_path: HTTP request -> CommonFilter (URL normalization only) -> WebContextFilter (thread-local setup only) -> DispatcherServlet -> REST service method (no auth check)
findings.title: dpfaulthorizonservice webapp lacks global authentication filter - all REST endpoints are unauthenticated
findings.message: The dpfaulthorizonservice web.xml only configures CommonFilter (URL normalization) and WebContextFilter (thread-local context) on /*, with no authentication or authorization filter. AuthorizeFilter, AuthFilter, URLAuthFilter, and SecurityFilter exist in the codebase but are NOT deployed. The enable_local config file is absent, meaning even if AuthorizeFilter were deployed, it would bypass all checks. All REST endpoints under /rest/* are served by DispatcherServlet without any auth interception.

findings.evidence: Line 80-82: innerRequest() directly returns this.doRequest(requestParam) without calling MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps(). Compare with request() at line 72-78 which calls checkAuthOps before doRequest. The doRequest method at line 84 calls distributeInstances which uses BackendErClient.distributeRequest and BackendErClient.invokeRequest.
findings.title: DPFaultRequestInterface.innerRequest endpoint acts as unauthenticated HTTP request proxy
findings.message: DPFaultRequestInterfaceDelegateImpl.innerRequest() directly calls doRequest() without any authentication check, while the sibling method request() properly calls MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps(). The innerRequest endpoint at /dpfault/tool/inner/request accepts a RequestParam containing url, method, params, and nodeIds, then forwards the request to backend instances via BackendErClient. This creates an unauthenticated SSRF-like proxy that can send requests to internal services.

findings.evidence: Line 68-70: recoveryModel() calls this.recoveryModelService.tryRecoveryModel(false) directly with no MutilUserAuthCheck. RecoveryModelService.tryRecoveryModel() at line 34-39 schedules execRecoveryModel which calls RuntimeExec.exec(RECOVERY_MODEL_CMD) where RECOVERY_MODEL_CMD = {'/bin/bash', RECOVERY_MODEL_SCRIPT}.
data_flow_path: GET /rest/dpfault/tool/recovery/model -> DPFaultRequestInterface.recoveryModel() -> delegate.recoveryModel() -> recoveryModelService.tryRecoveryModel(false) -> EXECUTOR.schedule(execRecoveryModel) -> RuntimeExec.exec('/bin/bash recovery_model.sh')
findings.title: DPFaultRequestInterface.recoveryModel endpoint triggers shell command execution without authentication
findings.message: DPFaultRequestInterfaceDelegateImpl.recoveryModel() calls recoveryModelService.tryRecoveryModel(false) without any authentication check. RecoveryModelService.tryRecoveryModel() schedules execution of recovery_model.sh via RuntimeExec.exec(). This allows unauthenticated users to trigger the recovery model script, which performs system recovery operations.

findings.evidence: 1) DPFaultRequestInterfaceDelegateImpl.request() at line 72-78 calls MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps() but still forwards user-controlled URL. 2) checkRequestMethod() at line 131: ConfigUtils.getErWhileList().stream().noneMatch(requestUrl::contains) - substring contains() check. 3) Whitelist entries from subhealth.properties are path fragments: /rest/om/iot/medroute/v1/device/connection/repair, /rest/dpfault/task/v1/check, /rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb, /rest/om/iot/medroute/v1/bin/dualchannel/double-master-recovery, /rest/dpfault/v1/isolateKafka. 4) Bypass payload: http://attacker.com/rest/dpfault/v1/check - the substring /rest/dpfault/v1/check is present in the URL. 5) BackendRestClient.createAdminHeader() injects admin credentials at line 86-91.
data_flow_path: DPFaultRequestInterfaceDelegateImpl.request() -> MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps() [auth check but URL still user-controlled]
BackendRestClient.invokeMethod() -> RestAdapter.get/post/put/delete(url, params, options) [outbound HTTP with user URL and admin creds]
findings.title: SSRF via request endpoint with weak contains() whitelist bypass and admin credential auto-injection
findings.message: The /dpfault/tool/request endpoint accepts a user-controlled URL via RequestParam.url and forwards it via BackendErClient/BackendRestClient. Although this endpoint has authentication (MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps), the whitelist validation in checkRequestMethod() uses String.contains() which can be bypassed. The whitelist entries are path fragments (e.g., /rest/dpfault/v1/check) validated against the user URL via substring matching. An attacker can bypass this by embedding the whitelisted path fragment in the URL path, query string, or fragment: e.g., http://evil.com/rest/dpfault/v1/check or http://evil.com?x=/rest/dpfault/v1/check. Additionally, BackendRestClient.invokeMethod() auto-injects admin credentials (x-user-name: admin, x-user-id: 1, x-user-roles: 1&2), enabling authenticated admin-level SSRF to internal APIs.

findings.evidence: Line 76-78: if ('permit'.equals(FileCache.getInstance().getCommonConfigsBean().getXmlEntity())) { sax = new SAXReader(); sax.setEntityResolver((publicId, systemId) -> new InputSource()); } else { sax = new WSFSaxRead(); }. The 'permit' branch lacks setFeature('http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl', true) and other XXE-safe features. WSFSaxRead (the 'reject' branch) correctly sets disallow-doctype-decl=true, external-general-entities=false, external-parameter-entities=false.
data_flow_path: HTTP request body -> ParamCheckFilter.doFilter (line 98) -> getRequestParam (line 441: new XmlParameter(request, bodyData, requestUrl))
SAXReader.read(reader) (line 82) with incomplete XXE protections when xmlEntity='permit'
findings.http_path: /* (ParamCheckFilter intercepts all requests with XML content type)
findings.title: XXE via SAXReader when xml-entity config is set to permit
findings.message: When the xml-entity configuration is set to 'permit', XMLProcessUtil.getXMLDocument creates a plain SAXReader without disabling DTD processing or external entities. Although an EntityResolver returning an empty InputSource is set, this only partially mitigates XXE: it blocks external entity content resolution (preventing file read/SSRF) but does not disable DTD declaration processing, leaving the parser vulnerable to DTD-based DoS attacks (e.g., Billion Laughs/XML Bomb) and parameter entity expansion. The default configuration is 'reject' which uses the secure WSFSaxRead class, but an administrator can change this to 'permit' via the xml-entity config attribute.

findings.evidence: HealthcheckServiceDelegateImpl has no import of MutilUserAuthCheck and no auth check calls. All methods directly delegate to taskCheckService without any authorization verification.
findings.title: HealthcheckService endpoints expose health check data without authentication
findings.message: HealthcheckServiceDelegateImpl provides health check data endpoints (band-width-data, fifteen-minute-data, middle-ware-data, one-minute-data, two-minute-data) without any authentication or authorization check. The class does not import or use MutilUserAuthCheck. Combined with the missing global auth filter, these endpoints expose internal monitoring data to unauthenticated users.

findings.evidence: DataCollectionMapper.xml line 475-477: <if test="orderByClause != null and orderByClause != ''">${orderByClause}</if>. The buildOrderByClause method (line 314-328) uses a switch expression on sortKey.toLowerCase() that maps to fixed constants: PACKAGE_SIZE->file_size, PROGRESS->progress, default->create_time; and sort direction is limited to ASC or DESC. No direct exploitation is possible with the current code path, but the ${} pattern bypasses MyBatis parameterization entirely.
data_flow_path: ScenariosCollectionServiceDelegateImpl.getTasks(context, taskId, page, pageSize, sort, sortKey, type, name, status)
buildOrderByClause(sort, sortKey) -> whitelist-sanitized ORDER BY string
findings.title: MyBatis ${} unsafe interpolation in ORDER BY clause (whitelist-sanitized but not parameterized)
findings.message: The findTasksWithPagination query in DataCollectionMapper.xml uses ${orderByClause} for string interpolation in the ORDER BY clause. While the current caller (ScenariosCollectionServiceDelegateImpl.buildOrderByClause) applies a whitelist that restricts the column name to one of three hardcoded values (create_time, file_size, progress) and the sort direction to ASC or DESC, the MyBatis ${} pattern is fundamentally unsafe. If the calling code is modified or new callers are added, SQL injection becomes immediately exploitable. The ORDER BY clause should use a parameterized approach or the whitelist should be enforced at the mapper level.

findings.evidence: HttpUtils.paresHeader (line 36-52) stores request header values into Context ThreadLocal without sanitization. HttpUtils.addHeader(HttpServletResponse) (line 125-149) writes Context values to response headers via setHeader. DispatcherServlet.beginTrack() (line 322) calls paresHeader, DispatcherServlet.execute() (line 313) calls addHeader. No CRLF filtering exists in Context class.
data_flow_path: Request headers (x-trace-id, x-full-chain, x-router-isolate, x-span-id, x-parent-id, x-sysprops-sampling) -> HttpUtils.paresHeader() -> Context ThreadLocal -> HttpUtils.addHeader(response) -> HttpServletResponse.setHeader()
findings.title: HTTP Header Reflection/CRLF Injection via DispatcherServlet Framework Trace Headers
findings.message: HttpUtils.paresHeader reads x-trace-id, x-full-chain, x-router-isolate, x-span-id, x-parent-id, x-sysprops-sampling, x-reference-id from incoming HttpServletRequest with no CRLF filtering or validation. HttpUtils.addHeader(HttpServletResponse) then writes these same values directly into the HTTP response headers via response.setHeader(). This header reflection affects all endpoints routed through DispatcherServlet, as beginTrack() calls paresHeader() before any business logic, and execute() calls addHeader(response) for every request. While Tomcat 10.1.x filters CRLF in setHeader(), per judgment rules servlet container-level CRLF filtering should not be relied upon as the sole defense.

findings.evidence: DownloadUtil.java line 69-74: User-Agent detection triggers bypass of URLEncoder.encode. Line 72: fileNameByWriter = new String(fileName.getBytes(DEFAULT_ENCODE), ISO_8859_1_ENCODE) -- no CRLF encoding. Line 74: resp.setHeader(CONTENT_DISPOSITION, CONTENT_DISPOSITION_VAL_PRE + fileNameByWriter). ScenariosCollectionServiceDelegateImpl.java line 420: StringUtils.isBlank check on taskId (no CRLF validation).
data_flow_path: taskId (request parameter) -> ScenariosCollectionServiceDelegateImpl.downloadCollectionPkg() -> DownloadUtil.downloadFile(path, taskId+'.zip', resp, req)
fileName = taskId+'.zip' -> (Firefox/Chrome branch) fileNameByWriter = new String(fileName.getBytes('utf-8'), 'iso8859-1')
findings.title: CRLF Injection in Content-Disposition Header via DownloadUtil (Firefox/Chrome branch bypasses URLEncoder)
findings.message: In DownloadUtil.downloadFile(), when the User-Agent header contains 'firefox' or 'chrome', the fileName bypasses URLEncoder.encode() and is processed as new String(fileName.getBytes('utf-8'), 'iso8859-1'), which preserves CRLF bytes unchanged. The result is concatenated into the Content-Disposition response header. The taskId parameter from downloadCollectionPkg is user-controlled (only validated with isBlank, no CRLF or numeric check) and directly becomes part of the fileName as taskId + '.zip'.

findings.title: SSRF Whitelist Bypass -> Command Injection -> RCE
findings.message: SSRF with weak whitelist bypass may chain to an internal command injection endpoint. If the proxy auto-injects admin credentials, the attacker gains authenticated RCE. Reachability from application code requires review.

amplifier.evidence: 1) DPFaultRequestInterfaceDelegateImpl.request() at line 72-78 calls MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps() but still forwards user-controlled URL. 2) checkRequestMethod() at line 131: ConfigUtils.getErWhileList().stream().noneMatch(requestUrl::contains) - substring contains() check. 3) Whitelist entries from subhealth.properties are path fragments: /rest/om/iot/medroute/v1/device/connection/repair, /rest/dpfault/task/v1/check, /rest/dpfault/v1/switchoverDb, /rest/om/iot/medroute/v1/bin/dualchannel/double-master-recovery, /rest/dpfault/v1/isolateKafka. 4) Bypass payload: http://attacker.com/rest/dpfault/v1/check - the substring /rest/dpfault/v1/check is present in the URL. 5) BackendRestClient.createAdminHeader() injects admin credentials at line 86-91.
data_flow_path: DPFaultRequestInterfaceDelegateImpl.request() -> MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps() [auth check but URL still user-controlled]
BackendRestClient.invokeMethod() -> RestAdapter.get/post/put/delete(url, params, options) [outbound HTTP with user URL and admin creds]
amplifier.title: SSRF via request endpoint with weak contains() whitelist bypass and admin credential auto-injection
amplifier.message: The /dpfault/tool/request endpoint accepts a user-controlled URL via RequestParam.url and forwards it via BackendErClient/BackendRestClient. Although this endpoint has authentication (MutilUserAuthCheck.checkAuthOps), the whitelist validation in checkRequestMethod() uses String.contains() which can be bypassed. The whitelist entries are path fragments (e.g., /rest/dpfault/v1/check) validated against the user URL via substring matching. An attacker can bypass this by embedding the whitelisted path fragment in the URL path, query string, or fragment: e.g., http://evil.com/rest/dpfault/v1/check or http://evil.com?x=/rest/dpfault/v1/check. Additionally, BackendRestClient.invokeMethod() auto-injects admin credentials (x-user-name: admin, x-user-id: 1, x-user-roles: 1&2), enabling authenticated admin-level SSRF to internal APIs.

target.evidence: COMMON_COMMAND = 'bash,-c,source /opt/oss/manager/bin/engr_profile.sh && ' followed by string concatenation of params.getUser(), params.getName(), params.getOutput(), params.getCmd(), params.getStartTime(), params.getEndTime(). buildCmdCommand() appends '-cmd ' + params.getCmd(). The result is split(',') and passed to ProcessBuilder.
data_flow_path: CollectionTask.buildCmdCommand(params) -> concatenates params.getUser(), params.getName(), params.getOutput(), params.getCmd(), params.getStartTime(), params.getEndTime() into shell command string
target.title: Command Injection via bash -c in CollectionTask (cmd collection)
target.message: CollectionTask.buildCmdCommand concatenates HTTP-controlled fields from CollectorCmdParams (user, name, output, cmd, startTime, endTime) directly into a shell command string executed via ProcessBuilder with 'bash -c'. The 'cmd' field is the most dangerous as it explicitly represents a command to execute. No sanitization is applied. Shell metacharacters in any parameter enable arbitrary command injection.

findings.chain_name: SSRF Whitelist Bypass -> Command Injection -> RCE

task_summary.supervisor_rationale: All 8 hunters delegated: code_execution (431 COMMAND_EXECUTION candidates, 2254 DYNAMIC_LOADING candidates), authorization (20 FILTER_ENTRY + 5 SERVLET_ENTRY candidates), sql_injection (3 SQL_EXECUTION candidates + 20 stored), ssrf (outbound HTTP with weak whitelist), unsafe_parsing (17 XML_PARSE candidates), file_operations (35 FILE_WRITE candidates), http_output (435 HTTP_HEADER_WRITE + 20 HTTP_RESPONSE_WRITE + 3 HTTP_REDIRECT candidates), component_vulns (jackson-databind + log4j dependency review). Code execution, authorization, SSRF, unsafe parsing, file operations and component vulns are mandatory per rules. SQL injection and HTTP output included due to non-zero candidate counts.
